q1.prompt: A 30-year-old man participates in a research study where his cardiac parameters are measured via echocardiography. If he is found to have an ejection fraction of 0.25 and an end-systolic volume (ESV) of 150 mL, what is his calculated end-diastolic volume (EDV)?

options.label: 175 mL
185 mL
190 mL
200 mL
225 mL

q2.prompt: On a standard left ventricular pressure-volume loop, which specific point or segment represents the mechanical event associated with the generation of the first heart sound (S1)?

options.label: Point 1: Mitral valve closure at the end of diastole
Point 2: Aortic valve opening at the start of ejection
Point 3: Aortic valve closure at the end of systole
Point 4: Mitral valve opening at the start of filling
The segment between Point 2 and Point 3

q3.prompt: A 75-year-old patient with long-standing hypertension presents with left ventricular hypertrophy. During auscultation, a low-frequency fourth heart sound (S4) is detected; which mechanical event in the pressure-volume relationship is responsible for this vibration?

options.label: Rapid passive filling of the ventricle during early diastole
Atrial contraction forcing blood into a stiff, non-compliant ventricle
Sudden closure of the semilunar valves at the start of relaxation
Turbulent flow across a stenosed aortic valve during peak systole
Rebound of the ventricular walls following isovolumetric relaxation

q4.prompt: A patient has a resting oxygen consumption (V_(O2)) of 250 mL/min, a peripheral arterial O_2 content of 0.20 mL O_2/mL blood, and a mixed venous O_2 content of 0.15 mL O_2/mL blood. If his heart rate is 72 beats/min, what is his calculated stroke volume?

options.label: 55.5 mL/beat
60.2 mL/beat
64.8 mL/beat
67.5 mL/beat
69.4 mL/beat

q5.prompt: Analysis of a Wiggers diagram reveals the point where the left ventricular pressure falls just below the left atrial pressure. Which mechanical event is initiated at this specific crossover point?

options.label: The closure of the aortic valve and the start of diastole
The onset of isovolumetric contraction following the QRS complex
The beginning of the reduced ejection phase as ventricular power wanes
The opening of the mitral valve to begin rapid ventricular filling
The closure of the mitral valve resulting in the first heart sound

q6.prompt: During a physical examination, a healthy volunteer is asked to take a deep inspiration. The clinician notes a distinct splitting of the second heart sound (S2); which analysis of right ventricular dynamics explains this phenomenon?

options.label: Increased intrathoracic pressure decreases venous return to the right heart
The pulmonic valve closes earlier due to decreased right ventricular volume
Decreased intrathoracic pressure prolongs the ejection time of the left ventricle
Rapid filling of the right atrium causes a premature tricuspid closure
Increased venous return to the right heart prolongs its ejection time, delaying pulmonic closure

q7.prompt: If a patient experiences a sudden and significant increase in mean arterial pressure (afterload), how will the left ventricular pressure-volume loop be altered in the next cycle?

options.label: The loop will shift to the left with a decreased end-systolic volume
The stroke volume will increase due to the higher pressure gradient
The end-systolic volume will increase while the stroke volume decreases
The end-diastolic volume will immediately decrease to compensate
The area of the loop will decrease while the ejection fraction increases

q8.prompt: A patient is diagnosed with a dilated, failing heart following a viral myocarditis. According to Laplace's Law, why does this patient's myocardium require significantly more oxygen to eject the same stroke volume as a healthy heart?

options.label: Increased wall thickness reduces the efficiency of oxygen diffusion
Decreased end-diastolic volume reduces the number of cross-bridges formed
Increased ventricular radius requires greater wall tension to generate the same pressure
Higher heart rates are required to maintain a constant cardiac output
The lack of troponin C sensitivity requires a higher threshold for contraction

q9.prompt: An analysis of cardiac and vascular function curves shows the intersection point representing the steady-state operating point of the heart. If a patient develops acute heart failure, how will the intersection of these curves shift?

options.label: Shift upward and to the left (increased CO, decreased RAP)
Shift upward and to the right (increased CO, increased RAP)
Shift to a new point with increased CO and unchanged RAP
Shift downward and to the right (decreased CO, increased RAP)
Shift downward and to the left (decreased CO, decreased RAP)

q10.prompt: A 68-year-old patient presents with easy fatigability and crepitant rales in the lung bases. Upon auscultation, an S3 heart sound is heard; which underlying physiological state does this sound indicate in this adult?

options.label: Ventricular volume overload or congestive heart failure
Normal physiological variant associated with high cardiac reserve
Presence of an atrial septal defect causing a left-to-right shunt
Premature closure of the mitral valve due to high atrial pressure
Decreased velocity of blood flow through the aortic valve

q11.prompt: On a standard ECG and pressure tracing, which phase of the ventricular cardiac cycle follows immediately after the completion of the QRS complex?

options.label: Rapid ventricular filling
Reduced ventricular ejection
Isovolumetric relaxation
Atrial systole
Isovolumetric contraction

q12.prompt: A clinician administers a therapeutic dose of Digoxin to a patient with systolic dysfunction. Which change on the ventricular pressure-volume loop would most accurately demonstrate the positive inotropic effect of this drug?

options.label: A shift of the end-systolic point to the right (higher volume)
A shift of the end-systolic point to the left (lower volume)
An increase in end-diastolic volume with a constant stroke volume
A decrease in the vertical height of the isovolumetric contraction segment
A widening of the loop caused primarily by a shift in the filling segment

q13.prompt: On a jugular venous pulse (JVP) tracing, the "c" wave is observed as a small positive deflection. Which mechanical event of the right heart is responsible for the appearance of this wave?

options.label: The contraction of the right atrium following the P wave
The rapid filling of the right ventricle during early diastole
The bulging of the tricuspid valve into the right atrium during isovolumetric contraction
The peak ejection of blood from the right ventricle into the pulmonary artery
The passive filling of the right atrium against a closed tricuspid valve

q14.prompt: A patient is diagnosed with third-degree (complete) heart block. During the assessment of the jugular venous pulse, the clinician observes "cannon a-waves"; what is the physiological explanation for these exaggerated waves?

options.label: The right ventricle is contracting during the rapid filling phase
The tricuspid valve is stenosed, requiring a more forceful atrial contraction
The right atrium is fibrillating, causing erratic pressure spikes
The pulmonic valve is closing prematurely due to low stroke volume
The right atrium contracts while the tricuspid valve is closed during ventricular systole

q15.prompt: Using the Fick principle, a student calculates the cardiac output for a patient with an O2consumption of 500 mL/min, an arterial O2content of 24 mL/dL, and a mixed venous O2  content of 16 mL/dL. What is the cardiac output in L/min?

options.label: 4.15 L/min
6.25 L/min
8.00 L/min
10.4 L/min
12.5 L/min

q16.prompt: A patient is found to have a moderate aortic valve regurgitation. Which set of hemodynamic shifts in the arterial pressure profile would be most characteristic of this valvular condition?

options.label: Decreased diastolic pressure, increased systolic pressure, and wide pulse pressure
Increased diastolic pressure, decreased systolic pressure, and narrow pulse pressure
Normal diastolic pressure, increased systolic pressure, and narrow pulse pressure
Decreased diastolic pressure, decreased systolic pressure, and narrow pulse pressure
Increased diastolic pressure, increased systolic pressure, and wide pulse pressure

q17.prompt: In a patient with moderate aortic valve stenosis, the clinician notes that the peak pressure in the left ventricle is 215 mm Hg, while the peak pressure in the aorta is only 100 mm Hg. What is the primary hemodynamic consequence of this large pressure gradient across the valve?

options.label: Preload of the left ventricle is significantly reduced
Afterload on the left ventricular myocardium is severely increased
The ejection fraction of the left ventricle increases to compensate
Total peripheral resistance decreases to facilitate aortic flow
The diastolic filling time is prolonged to allow for the high output

q18.prompt: During the auscultation of a 45-year-old male, a "blowing" holosystolic murmur is heard at the apex, radiating to the axilla. Which mechanical dysfunction is most likely, and how does it affect the left ventricular pressure-volume loop?

options.label: Mitral stenosis; the loop shifts to the left with decreased filling
Aortic stenosis; the height of the loop increases during ejection
Mitral regurgitation; the isovolumetric segments are lost due to backflow into the atrium
Mitral regurgitation; the end-diastolic volume is increased, but the total stroke volume decreases significantly.
Aortic regurgitation; the loop widens as the ventricle fills from two directions

q19.prompt: In the steady state of a healthy cardiovascular system, the cardiac output of the left ventricle must equal that of the right ventricle. Which intrinsic mechanism is primarily responsible for equalizing these outputs over time?

options.label: The baroreceptor reflex adjusting the heart rate beat-by-beat
Arteriolar resistance shifts in the pulmonary circulation
Selective sympathetic outflow to the less efficient ventricle
Variation in total peripheral resistance to match right heart afterload
The Frank-Starling law matching stroke volume to changes in venous return

q20.prompt: A patient exhibits a profound sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 190 beats/min. Why does his cardiac output eventually fail to increase linearly with the heart rate at this extreme value?

options.label: The diastolic filling time is compromised, resulting in a significantly reduced EDV
The systolic ejection time is prolonged, causing blood to backup in the atria
The absolute refractory period of the ventricle increases, preventing contraction
The velocity of blood flow becomes turbulent at high frequencies
Myocardial contractility is inhibited by the excessive rate of action potentials

q21.prompt: A 21-year-old male patient has a maximum cardiac output of 16 L/min and a cardiac reserve of 300%. What is his calculated resting cardiac output?

options.label: 3 L/min
4 L/min
5.33 L/min
6 L/min
8 L/min

q22.prompt: Analysis of the jugular venous pulse reveals a "v" wave that peaks just before the opening of the tricuspid valve. What physiological event is responsible for the rising phase of this wave?

options.label: The contraction of the right atrium against a closed tricuspid valve
The bulging of the tricuspid valve during right ventricular contraction
The filling of the right atrium from the vena cava while the tricuspid valve is closed
The rapid emptying of the right atrium once the tricuspid valve opens
The transmission of the aortic pressure wave into the superior vena cava

q23.prompt: A patient with patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) is found to have a significantly widened pulse pressure. What is the primary hemodynamic reason for the decrease in his diastolic blood pressure?

options.label: Reduced cardiac output during the diastolic phase
Continuous runoff of blood from the aorta into the pulmonary artery
Increased compliance of the systemic arterial tree
Inhibition of the baroreceptor reflex by high aortic pressure
Backward flow of blood from the aorta into the left ventricle

q24.prompt: A student examines cardiac function and venous return curves plotted on the same graph. If a drug is administered that significantly increases myocardial contractility without changing blood volume, to which point will the operating point shift from the control?

options.label: Up and to the right
Down and to the left
Up and to the left
Down and to the right
No shift will occur, as CO and VR must remain equal
